card.intuition: A monotonic deque tracks candidates for the window maximum — new larger elements evict smaller ones from the back, and elements that slide out of the window are removed from the front.
card.analogy: A VIP list for the best k seats in a venue. When a more important guest arrives, less-important guests behind them lose their reserved spot. Guests whose booking expired (left the window) are removed from the front automatically.
card.time_complexity: O(n) — each element is added and removed from the deque at most once
when_to_use: Maximum (or minimum) of every contiguous subarray of size k
DP optimization where dp[i] = max(dp[j]) for j in [i-k, i-1]
Whenever a brute-force O(nk) sliding window can be reduced to O(n)
gotchas: Store indices in the deque, not values — you need indices to check whether the front has expired
Evict from back when new value >= back value (use >= to remove stale equal elements, avoiding a leftover that shouldn't be max)
Evict from front when front index <= i - k (not < i - k)

exercise.prompt: Find the minimum (not maximum) of every sliding window of size k — same deque technique, opposite comparison.
hints: The minimum of the current window is always at the front of the deque: nums[dq[0]]
This is the same structure as sliding-window-max but with the comparison flipped (evict larger instead of smaller)
exercise.test_code:
assert sliding_window_min([1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7], 3) == [-1,-3,-3,-3,3,3]
assert sliding_window_min([1,2,3,4,5], 3)           == [1,2,3]
assert sliding_window_min([5,4,3,2,1], 2)           == [4,3,2,1]
assert sliding_window_min([4], 1)                   == [4]
assert sliding_window_min([2,1,5,3], 2)             == [1,1,3]
print('PASS')
